topic: A person finds their childhood home listed for sale online
raw_output:
[story 1]

The notification blinked on the screen, a stark, unwelcome intrusion into the otherwise mundane scroll of the afternoon. *For Sale: 14 Willow Creek Lane.* A cold knot tightened in my stomach, a familiar, nauseating clench. I stared at the listing photo—the peeling paint, the overgrown rose bushes—and felt my cheeks flush hot. It was the house where I’d spent every summer, the backdrop to all those carefree days. Now, it was just a commodity, a price tag waiting to be slapped on it. I quickly minimized the browser, my fingers trembling slightly as I gripped the edge of the desk. Why was I even looking at this? The memory, usually a warm blanket, suddenly felt like a spotlight exposing something deeply flawed. I pushed back from the computer, the chair scraping loudly against the floor, and walked toward the window, needing the harsh, unfiltered light to burn away the sudden, intense discomfort blooming in my chest.

[story 2]

I reread the address three times. Fourteen Willow Creek Lane. My breath hitched, shallow and quick. It wasn't just a house; it was the repository of my earliest self, the place where I felt utterly safe. Now, seeing it advertised online, a glossy picture juxtaposed with the reality of its decay, made my throat constrict. A wave of intense self-reproach washed over me, heavy and suffocating. I felt my shoulders hunch inward, pulling my arms tight across my chest as if trying to physically shrink away from the information. "No," I whispered to the empty room, the sound thin and brittle. I slammed the laptop shut, the noise echoing too loudly in the quiet apartment. I paced the small space, my movements jerky and agitated, unable to settle until the physical tension began to ebb, leaving behind a residue of deep unease.

[story 3]

The email subject line was deceptively simple: "Opportunity at Willow Creek." My hands started shaking so badly I had to press them flat against my thighs to steady them. Opening the listing felt like picking at an open wound. Every detail—the faded porch swing, the sagging roofline—sent a sharp, unwelcome jolt through me. I watched the cursor blink relentlessly on the 'Contact Agent' button, a paralysis gripping my limbs. A deep, internal churning began, a sensation of profound inadequacy rising up from my core. I quickly navigated away, closing the tab with unnecessary force, the resulting snap of the laptop lid sounding disproportionately loud. I stood motionless for a long moment, breathing in ragged, shallow gasps, trying desperately to reassert some semblance of control over the unexpected, heavy feeling settling over me.
